# Book-level distillation — harmonic-mechanisms-vol-1

_Aggregated from per-chapter summaries under `summaries/`._

## Central Thesis

George Van Eps's *Harmonic Mechanisms for Guitar, Vol. 1* is built on a single, uncompromising architectural claim: the guitar - specifically the seven-string guitar - is a polyphonic instrument in the keyboard sense, and the entire curriculum exists to install on the fretboard the 'lap-piano' capacity to sustain three and four genuinely independent contrapuntal lines at once. Chapter 1's front matter declares the method polyphonic-only - there are no single-voice studies anywhere in the volume because every example carries two or more voices - and frames the work as the engineering of four interlocking training objectives: independent finger control, multi-thought control, independent harmonic chromatic notational selectivity, and the past/present/logical-future/surprise voice-trajectory awareness the student must apply to every voice in every chord. The book is unapologetically systematic over expressive: chords are not catalogued by name or function but generated as the frozen residue of moving voices ('arpeggios and chords are frozen/broken versions of each other'), and harmony is treated as the incidental coalescence of independent lines (Chapters 13-14 make this explicit by declaring bass and triad to be separate identities that merely happen to align). The volume's load-bearing claim is that this counterpoint-on-guitar ideal is reachable only through chromatic selectivity - 'CHROMATICS, CHROMATICS, AND MORE CHROMATICS' is named as the series' guiding obsession in Chapter 6 - and through a small set of named physical mechanisms (standing on notes, the fifth-finger principle, arched-finger hammers, mechanical-linkage finger teaming) that engineer keyboard-style sustain into a fretted instrument. The seven-string register is integral, not incidental: the heavy bass it affords is justified by the tonal-chemistry / overtone-density argument in Chapter 1 that light upper triads need heavy lower foundation to balance, and the broken/divided string-set notation introduced in Chapters 6 and 12 only becomes necessary because the seventh string opens non-adjacent voicing possibilities that the six-string instrument cannot reach.

## Pedagogical Arc

The book moves through a deliberate seven-stage progression in which each stage installs the vocabulary and physical control the next stage requires. Chapter 1 (front matter) installs the foundational physical rules - thumb behind the neck, fatigue-factor economy of pressing only the strings that sound, four-finger walking-action alternation with no repeated fingers, and 'standing on notes' (arched-fingertip plus barre to lock sustained tones while other fingers move) - along with the conceptual scaffolding of selectivity, tonal chemistry, delayed coupling, and the past/present/logical-future/surprise voice-trajectory framework. Chapter 2 enthrones the triad as the universal substrate of every subsequent study and drills 1st-inversion, 2nd-inversion, and root-position triad scales through major, harmonic-minor, and melodic-minor systems, transposed via the descending cycle of fifths (preferred for fingering variety and because Western harmony is gravitationally pulled downward by fifths); it also installs the outer-voice-anchor / deletion rule (the strong sixth between top and bottom of a 1st-inversion triad lets the middle be deleted to compress six-note compounded triads into four-note hand-grips) and the 'hint/suggestion of a tone' inferred-note device for implying notes the hand cannot physically grip. Chapter 3 is the short 'mixed minors' interlude that combines melodic-minor ascent against harmonic-minor descent and reverses the roles across all three triad inversions, training the student to extend printed examples across the full fingerboard rather than treat the page as the assignment. Chapter 4 is the first deep mechanical chapter and teaches 'reductions' - the systematic conversion between triads and the third-to-sixth intervals embedded inside them - worked in all four directions (3-up-to-6, 3-down-to-6, 6-up-to-3, 6-down-to-3) through all three modes, with the load-bearing 'scales within scales' insight (the 3rd-to-6th intervals are themselves scales hidden inside the triad scales of Chapter 2) and the anchor-and-move drill (sustain the whole note while the other voice runs the scale up and down) as the foundation of polyphonic independence. Chapter 5 animates those sixths with the fixed numeric upper-voice pattern 1-5-3-1-5-1-3-5 while the sixths open out into tenths, building each measure ('station') as a dual-triad construction (inferred 1st-inversion triad with middle deleted, then arpeggiated root-position triad) and installing the chapter-specific 'no barres' doctrine - the barre is too harplike and lets tones ring uncontrollably, so individual notes must be sounded by arched-finger hammers that walk on the fingers. Chapter 6 installs the notational and cognitive infrastructure the rest of the volume depends on: the chiastic 'ears see, eyes hear' principle of mutually predictive shape and sound, the 'mutation' phenomenon naming why identical voicings change notation across string sets (traced to the single physical cause of the major third between B and G), and the full string-set symbol grammar including the broken-set marker. Chapter 7 builds chromatic triads against pedal tones and alternating bass, with the central claim that the chromatic third-to-tenth exercises generate every major, minor, diminished, and augmented scale and the signature single-finger chromatic walk (one finger sounds two or three successive half steps while the pedal stays held). Chapters 8-11 are the Super and Sub Series - the volume's structural heart - in which 'super' (raised one diatonic step) and 'sub' (lowered one step) voice motions are drilled across 1st-inversion (Chapter 8), 2nd-inversion (Chapter 9), and root-position triads (Chapter 10), then extended into open voicings with multi-voice motion (Chapter 11) where two voices move while a third is sustained full value. Chapter 12 introduces the Fifth Finger Principle - angling and arching the first finger so its tip and the upper inside of its third joint sound two notes on non-adjacent frets simultaneously - which unlocks the open-voicing arsenal, and consolidates the visualization doctrine (fingerboard cognition as geographic shape-recognition with multiple harmonic modes stacked in layers) and the chromatic-scale-as-universal-source thesis. Chapter 13 drills chromatic triad/bass studies through the Major and Minor Series cycled around the circle of fifths and in contrary motion, generated by 'mathematical mechanical motion without regard for the harmonic result,' with bass treated as an independent fourth voice often in a different key from the triad. Chapter 14 closes the volume with the 'blockout' - a graphic two-line study in which a series of major and minor triads in root, 1st, and 2nd inversions runs on the upper line against a completely independent chromatic bass on the lower line, organized into 'repeat order' and 'mixed order' taxonomies, with an explicit construction recipe (three keys a major third apart, repeat-order inversions, chromatic bass from any starting step) so the student can generate their own.

## Characteristic Van Eps Moves

A dense set of named operational moves recurs across the volume and gives it its unmistakable signature. Standing on notes: the arched-fingertip-plus-barre combination that locks sustained tones full value while other fingers articulate independently - the load-bearing physical move behind every polyphonic study (Chapter 1, applied throughout). The fatigue factor: press only the strings that actually sound, never more (Chapter 1). The walking-action / no-repeated-fingers rule: four-finger alternation governs all articulation, with the structural concession that three-voice work cannot use full four-finger alternation and must fall back to partial two-finger alternation (Chapters 1-2). The outer-voice-anchor / deletion rule: when the outer sixth of a 1st-inversion triad is strong enough, delete the middle - this is what compresses six-note compounded triads into four-note hand-grips and is the harmonic engine of the inferred-triad voicings used from Chapter 2 onward. The hint/suggestion-of-a-tone inferred-note device: imply notes the hand cannot grip by sounding the surrounding voices so the ear fills the missing tone (Chapter 2). Delayed coupling: rapid successive partial chords are stitched by the ear into a single larger harmony - the temporal-compounding engine of the lap-piano illusion (Chapter 2). The descending cycle of fifths as the universal transposition order, preferred over chromatic key-shifts for both fingering variety and harmonic-gravity reasons (Chapters 2, 10). The reduction system: the systematic conversion between triads and their embedded thirds and sixths in all four directions, drilled across all three modes (Chapter 4). The anchor-and-move drill: sustain the whole note while the other voice runs the scale - the foundational polyphonic-independence exercise, with fingers 3 and 4 explicitly named as the load-bearing anchors (Chapter 4). The dual-triad station: each measure is built as inferred 1st-inversion triad with middle deleted in the first half plus arpeggiated root-position triad in the second half (Chapter 5). The 1-5-3-1-5-1-3-5 upper-voice pulsation pattern with top-voice emphasis driving the sixths-into-tenths studies (Chapter 5). Arched-finger hammers / 'walking on the fingers': individual notes sounded by arched fingertips rather than barre, because the barre is too harplike and lets tones ring uncontrollably (Chapter 5). The mutation phenomenon: identical voicings change notation across string sets because of the single physical cause of the major third between B and G - the named explanation behind the six-string-set fingering apparatus (Chapter 6). The string-set symbol grammar: slash-line notation with set number before and string count after (e.g., 1/B3 for broken three-string voicings, D1/4 and 1/D3 for divided sets) - the notational machinery without which the volume is unreadable (Chapters 6, 12). The single-finger chromatic walk: one finger smoothly sounds two or three successive half steps while the pedal stays held - the signature left-hand demand of the pedal-bass studies (Chapter 7). The super and sub motions: diatonic-step voice raises (super) and lowers (sub) read as melodic occurrences in the chromatic inner line, with the inversion-membership shift rule that every super or sub event lands the triad on another named inversion in the family (Chapters 8-11). The 'original inversion is the boss' doctrine: hold the named source inversion in mind at all times because identical notations carry different functional meaning depending on which inversion caused them (Chapters 8, 10). The four-string articulated triad: the single melodic step in the moving upper line is distributed across two strings instead of one, with the listener-side correctness test that only three tones should sound at any instant unless a hold-over is intended (Chapter 9). The second-finger hammer mechanic with mechanical-linkage thought line: the first joint of the second finger plays the double stop flattened then arches into hammer position at the precise moment, with the raising of the flattened joint and the lowering of the first finger behaving as if mechanically linked (Chapter 9). The four-key voice-change shorthand: voice changes are written every four keys of the cycle of fifths rather than all twelve, leaving the rest to student reconstruction (Chapter 10). The range-edge step-loss compensation: add a step at one end whenever one is lost at the other (Chapter 10). The arched-2nd-finger sustain: arched second finger holds the middle voice through open-voicing motion, with the cross (+) notational convention marking the deleted middle voice of the 1st-inversion triad that begins each station (Chapter 11). The Fifth Finger Principle: angle and arch the first finger so its tip and the upper inside of its third joint sound two non-adjacent notes simultaneously - the central left-hand contribution of the volume (Chapter 12). The mechanical-motion-regardless-of-harmonic-result generator: produce voicings by strict mathematical motion through the circle of fifths and contrary motion without regard for what chord results, then accept the resulting clashes (Chapter 13). The major-makes-minor / minor-makes-major identity: a 7th chord is heard as a triad of the opposite modality over a bass, with inversion arpeggios used to surface which familiar four-note chord is actually present (Chapter 13). The chameleon-chord doctrine: any voicing has many callings under varied conditions (Chapter 13). The bass-suffix chord-symbol notation (e.g., 3B for third-in-bass) without which the Major/Minor Series labels are unreadable (Chapter 13). And the blockout itself: a graphic two-line study with independent triad upper line and chromatic bass lower line, organized into repeat-order and mixed-order taxonomies and authorizing five named deviation techniques once the strict form is mastered - repeat the chord while bass continues, repeat the bass while chords continue, skip steps in either cycle, splice pleasing sections of different cycles, or move one line by a whole tone (Chapter 14).

## Sanctioned Departures and Bounded Exceptions

The method is otherwise rigorously universal - polyphonic-only, transpose everything through the descending cycle of fifths, drill bidirectionally, no single-voice studies, no barres in the Chapter 5 sixths studies, no open strings except where absolutely necessary because they break fingering-pattern transposability (the 'first finger replaces open strings' substitution being the standing workaround) - but it carries a small set of explicitly sanctioned departures. Three-voice work cannot use full four-finger alternation and must fall back to partial two-finger alternation, named as a structural limit rather than a defect (Chapter 2). Intentionally awkward fingerings are pedagogical features for agility, not errors (Chapters 2, 12). Substitute fingerings exist for very long second-interval spreads, but the student should attempt the original wide spread first as a stretch builder (Chapter 3). Register changes are sanctioned when a voice-pair exhausts physical range (Chapter 3). Mixed four-string and three-string fingerings within a single passage are authorized for smoothness (Chapter 9). Release of a sustained voice is permitted in the Chapter 11 open-voicing studies only as a last resort. Dissonance is reframed as a feature rather than a defect of chromatic contrary motion and pedal-bass writing - the student must not flinch from the inevitable clashes and must not 'fix' them during the learning stage (Chapters 13-14). And once the strict mathematical form of a blockout is mastered, five named deviation techniques are authorized for taming unwanted clashes (Chapter 14). Diagram-style fingerings and string-set markings are deliberately omitted from the blockouts so the student is forced to finger every one in as many positions as possible (Chapter 14).

## Aesthetic Axioms

A compact set of aesthetic claims underwrites every prescription in the book. Counterpoint on guitar is the artistic ideal: the guitar must be played as if it were a small keyboard, with two, three, or four independent voices sustained simultaneously, and every exercise in the volume is engineered to that end. Harmonic mechanism is itself a system, not a vocabulary - chords are the frozen residue of voice motion, and the curriculum drills the motion rather than the residue. Chromatic selectivity is the lifeline of music ('chromatics as the very life line of music,' Chapter 2; 'CHROMATICS, CHROMATICS, AND MORE CHROMATICS,' Chapter 6) - the chromatic scale is the universal source containing every scale, arpeggio, and chord in all twelve keys, and chromatic inner motion against sustained outer voices is the engine of every study from Chapter 6 onward. Tonal chemistry / register density is real and load-bearing: heavy seven-string bass against light upper triads is justified by overtone physics, and inner voices must be given room to be heard. Mind leads hands: selectivity is defined as chromatic fingerboard control with the mind leading the hands, fingerboard cognition is geographic and layered, and 'ears see, eyes hear' is the chiastic principle that shape and sound must be mutually predictive. Voice trajectory is composed: every voice in every chord has a past, a present, a logical future, and the possibility of surprise, and the student must apply this framework continuously. All chords are centers: every voicing is both a destination and a launchpad - there is no privileged tonic. Long reaches come from relaxed power and tendon control, not anatomy: tensing actually shortens reach (Chapters 7, 12), and reach is developed only gradually and in context. Hearing voice motion outranks executing it cleanly: in the Chapter 11 open-voicing studies the analytical step of hearing and analyzing the motion is co-equal with playing it. Slow correct first readings are mandatory because 'mistakes are habit forming' and wrong notes plant subconscious habits (Chapters 4, 8). Reading is not studying: real practice does not begin until the hands have memorized where they belong (Chapter 5). Practice hard shots first so easy shots take care of themselves (Chapter 12). Similar is not identical: near-duplicate exercises are deliberate studies of micro-differences rather than redundancy (Chapter 1). And the meta-axiom that the seven-string fingerboard must be visualized from above as a layered geographic space in which multiple harmonic modes are stacked on the same physical territory (Chapter 12).

## The Method as a Unit and a Note on Scope

Taken together, Volume 1 records an integrated polyphonic-guitar method with four interlocking commitments. First, the unit of study is the moving voice, not the chord: every exercise is a counterpoint study, every chord is the frozen residue of voice motion, and the load-bearing drills (reductions, sixths-with-upper-line-motion, super/sub series, blockouts) are all motion-of-independent-voices studies. Second, chromatic selectivity is the universal generator: chromatic inner lines against sustained outer anchors generate the full scale vocabulary, and the chromatic scale itself is named as the universal source. Third, the seven-string fingerboard is engineered through a small set of named physical mechanisms (standing on notes, arched-finger hammers, the single-finger chromatic walk, the second-finger hammer with mechanical-linkage finger teaming, the arched-2nd-finger sustain, and most centrally the Fifth Finger Principle) into a lap-piano-capable instrument, with the broken/divided string-set notation as the apparatus that lets non-adjacent three-string voicings be specified across the six string sets the seventh string makes available. Fourth, harmony is generated by mechanical mathematical motion through fixed transposition orders (the descending cycle of fifths above all, plus contrary motion and the circle of fifths in Chapters 13-14), with dissonance accepted as a feature of the system and the chameleon-chord, major-makes-minor, and bass-as-independent-fourth-voice doctrines licensing the analytical re-reading of every resulting voicing under multiple names. The whole sits inside an explicit systems-engineering stance: the four training objectives of Chapter 1 are the design specification, the named mechanisms are the implementation, the cycle-of-fifths drills and Super/Sub series and blockouts are the test suite, and the lap-piano polyphonic ideal is the acceptance criterion.

A note on scope and source. This volume is the seven-string method - Van Eps's own instrument - and the broken/divided string-set apparatus (six string sets including non-adjacent 'broken' sets like 1/B3 and divided sets like D1/4) presupposes the seventh string. Six-string players can execute much of the material but cannot reach the broken-set voicings as written. The 334-page source was OCR-acquired and chapter summaries occasionally reflect surface-level notational decoding rather than deep musical reasoning where the underlying OCR may have been imperfect; the volume's later chapters (the Super/Sub series across Chapters 8-11 in particular) carry more fingering and string-set detail than the front-matter and early-mechanical chapters convey, and the blockout charts of Chapter 14 are inherently graphic in a way that text summaries can only approximate. The distillation above reflects what the chapter summaries explicitly contain and does not extrapolate beyond them.


CHAPTER SUMMARIES:

# Chapter 1 — Front Matter: Foreword, General Remarks, Chemistry, Selectivity, Voicing and String-Set Charts

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch01.md`._

This front-matter chapter is foundational rather than exercise-driven: it installs the four training objectives that engineer the entire seven-string method — independent finger control, multi-thought control, and independent harmonic chromatic notational selectivity (Independent finger and multi-thought control) — and declares the method polyphonic-only, with no single-voice studies because every example carries two or more voices (No single-voice studies). Van Eps codifies the past/present/logical-future/surprise framework the student must apply to every voice in every chord (Voice trajectory in composition) and warns that the book's near-duplicate exercises are deliberate studies of micro-differences rather than redundancy (Similar is not identical). The chapter then lays down the load-bearing physical rules that the rest of the volume depends on: classical thumb-behind-the-neck posture (Thumb placement on neck), the economy 'fatigue factor' principle of pressing only the strings that sound (Fatigue factor), four-finger walking-action alternation with the corollary 'don't repeat fingers' (Walking action / alternation principle; Don't repeat fingers), and 'standing on notes' — the arched-fingertip + barre combination that locks down sustained tones while other fingers move and which enables the lap-piano polyphonic independence Van Eps is famous for (Standing on notes). Finally it introduces the central organizing concepts of the method — 'selectivity' as chromatic fingerboard control with mind leading hands (Selectivity defined), the tonal-chemistry/timbre-density rationale that justifies heavy seven-string bass under light upper triads (Tonal chemistry / register density), the aphorism that arpeggios and chords are frozen/broken versions of each other (Arpeggios are frozen chords), and the spacing-for-audibility rule that inner voices need room to be heard (Inner voices need room).

# Chapter 2 — The Mighty Triads: First/Second Inversion and Root-Position Triad Scales (Major, Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch02.md`._

Chapter 2 launches the actual exercise program by enthroning the triad as the universal substrate of every study in the book and then running 1st-inversion, 2nd-inversion, and root-position triad scales chromatically through the major, harmonic-minor, and melodic-minor systems (Triad as central unit; Triad names defined). Van Eps ties this directly to selectivity by declaring chromatics 'the very life line of music' (Chromatics as life line) and routes all transposition through the descending cycle of fifths rather than chromatic key-shifts, both for fingering variety and because Western harmony is itself gravitationally pulled downward by fifths (Descending cycle of fifths preferred; Gravitational descending harmony). The chapter installs several load-bearing voicing doctrines that recur throughout the volume: the outer-voice-anchor / deletion rule — the major or minor sixth between top and bottom of a 1st-inversion triad is so strong that the middle can be deleted, compressing six-note compounded triads into four-note hand-grips (Compounding triads / deletion rule); the 'hint/suggestion of a tone' inferred-note device for implying notes the hand can't physically grip (Hint / suggestion of a tone); overtone-aware voicing that justifies heavy bass against light upper triads (Overtones in voicing); and 'delayed coupling,' the temporal-compounding trick where rapid successive partial chords are stitched together by the ear into a single larger harmony — the engine of Van Eps's lap-piano illusion (Delayed coupling). The chapter also codifies the satellite-key duplication pattern as a transposition shortcut (Satellite key relationships), warns that intentionally awkward fingerings are pedagogical features for agility rather than errors (Intentionally awkward fingerings), and concedes the structural limit that three-voice work cannot use full four-finger alternation — partial two-finger alternation is the workaround (Three-voice alternation limit).

# Chapter 3 — Mixed Minors

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch03.md`._

This short chapter introduces the 'mixed minors' device — combining melodic-minor ascent in one voice against harmonic-minor descent in another, then reversing the roles so the harmonic form lives in the middle voice while the melodic form lives in the lower voice (Mixed minors definition). The chapter's exercise architecture is the matrix of all three triad inversions (1st, 2nd, root) crossed with melodic-up against harmonic-down (Mixed minors full inversion cycle), and Van Eps directs the student to extend every printed example across the full fingerboard rather than treat the page as the assignment (Extend full range). Two characteristic Van Eps mechanisms surface here: deliberate register-shifts when a voice-pair exhausts physical range (Register change device), and the discipline that substitute fingerings exist for very long second-interval spreads but the student should attempt the original wide spread first as a stretch builder (Substitute fingerings for wide spreads). The chapter closes by extending the chapter-1 fingerboard-mapping discipline downward into the inner voices: practical duplicate voicings must be hunted in the middle and lower voices, not just the upper line (Practical duplicates and inner-voice search).

# Chapter 4 — Reductions: 3-to-6 and 6-to-3 Triad Reductions (Major, Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch04.md`._

Chapter 4 is the volume's first deep mechanical chapter and teaches 'reductions' — the systematic conversion between triads and the third-to-sixth intervals embedded inside them — worked in all four directions (3rd up to 6th, 3rd down to 6th, 6th up to 3rd, 6th down to 3rd) through major, harmonic-minor, and melodic-minor systems (Reductions defined). The central conceptual move is 'scales within scales': the third-to-sixth intervals are themselves scales hidden inside the triad scales of chapter 2 (Scales within scales). The defining physical drill is the anchor-and-move pattern — sustain the whole note while the other voice runs the scale up and down — which Van Eps presents as the foundation of polyphonic independence (Hold the whole note while voice moves), with fingers 3 and 4 explicitly named as the load-bearing anchors of the reduction shapes (Third and fourth fingers as main stays) and the student told to analyze hand-shape geometry (long angles, slight angles, parallels, squares) as a study object in its own right (Analyze hand shapes). Several notational and practical rules are made load-bearing for the rest of the volume: the resolution chord at the end of each measure is a visual orientation marker not a played note, and string-set markings persist until explicitly changed — without grasping this the chapter is unreadable (Resolution chord for orientation only); open strings are avoided except where absolutely necessary because they break the fingering-pattern transposability that the method depends on (Open strings only when necessary); and the recurring 'first finger replaces open strings' substitution converts open-string voicings into chromatically-transposable shapes (First finger replaces open strings). Van Eps frames the slow-practice discipline as protection against muscle-memory errors with the aphorism 'mistakes are habit forming' (Mistakes are habit forming), and the chapter closes by directing the student to compound and mix the stations across major and minor to generate the full vocabulary from the printed seeds (Compound and mix the stations).

# Chapter 5 — Sixths with Upper-Line Motion (Full Range, All Three Modes)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch05.md`._

Chapter 5 takes the sixths developed in chapter 4 and animates them with a fixed numeric upper-voice pattern (1-5-3-1-5-1-3-5) while the sixths themselves open out into tenths, generating sixths-with-upper-line-motion exercises across all three modes and the full range of the instrument (Upper line motion pattern; Inverted pattern position of tenths). Each measure ('station') is built as a dual-triad construction: an inferred first-inversion triad with the middle note deleted in the first half, followed by an arpeggiated root-position triad in the second half — and Van Eps demands the analytic step of mapping every sixth back to its parent 1st- or 2nd-inversion triad and the major/minor tenths it implies before playing (Triads inferred inside the station; Identify the related triad). The chapter installs the chapter-specific physical doctrine that there are no barres, small or grand: the barre is too harplike and lets tones ring uncontrollably, so individual notes must be sounded by 'arched-finger hammers' that walk on the fingers for clean polyphonic articulation (No barres — walking on fingers; Arched finger hammers), and anchor half- and whole-notes must always be sustained for their full printed value while the other voices move (Sustain half and whole notes for full value). Two notational decoding rules are made load-bearing for reading every subsequent exercise: a single marked bar establishes a mechanism type rather than literal fingering (Finger marking convention), and in the melodic-form studies the ascending station numbers do not match the descending ones because the descent uses the relative major — station 1 ascending becomes station 6 descending (Ascending vs descending station numbering). The chapter closes the loop on study discipline with Van Eps's wedge between sight-reading and study: 'reading through exercises does not constitute study,' and real practice does not begin until the hands have memorized where they belong (Reading is not studying).

# Chapter 6 — Chromatic Triads and Chromatic Scales in Intervals

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch06.md`._

Chapter 6 introduces the notational and cognitive infrastructure that the rest of Van Eps's chromatic-triad work depends on, anchored by the lap-piano demand that sustained whole and half notes be held under full left-hand pressure while other fingers articulate the inner line independently (Sustain pressure while articulating). Articulation is governed by a strict simultaneity rule—left-hand placement and right-hand attack must arrive together, never with fingers pre-planted (Hand attack coordination)—and the sixths upper-line study is differentiated from surrounding material by its compressed one-octave spread versus the default tenth (Sixths upper line study). The load-bearing terminology arrives in three layers: Van Eps's chiastic doctrine that shape and sound are mutually predictive (Ears see, eyes hear) plus the prescription to visualize the fingerboard from above (Visualize fingerboard from above); the 'mutation' phenomenon naming why identical voicings change notation across string sets, traced to the single physical cause of the major third between B and G (Shape mutation across sets; Cause of notational variety); and the string-set symbol grammar itself—the slash-line notation with set number before and string count after, including the 'broken' set marker (e.g., 1/B3) that lets non-adjacent three-string voicings be specified (String set symbol system; Broken and divided sets). The chapter closes by declaring the series' guiding obsession—'CHROMATICS, CHROMATICS, AND MORE CHROMATICS' (Chromatics emphasis)—which frames every subsequent exercise as a study in chromatic inner motion.

# Chapter 7 — Chromatic Triads with Pedal and Alternating Bass

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch07.md`._

Chapter 7 builds chromatic triads against pedal tones and alternating bass lines, opening with Van Eps's thesis on extended fingerings—reach is a product of relaxed tendon control rather than hand size (Long reaches via relaxation)—developed only through gradual in-context practice rather than mechanical wedges (Develop reach gradually), with tensing identified as the counterproductive failure mode that actually shortens reach (Forcing shortens reach). The chapter's central drill is positioned as a universal scale-construction engine: chromatic third-to-tenth exercises, taken in all forms and positions, generate every major, minor, diminished, and augmented scale (Constructing all scales from chromatic exercises), and the student is explicitly authorized to fragment these written forms into custom 1-, 2-, or 4-note drills (Construct your own exercises). Two load-bearing practice rules govern execution—treat each measure as a separate exercise with the whole-note pedal sustained for the full bar (Treat each measure separately), and recognize that the sustained outer voice is itself describing the parent scale beneath the moving chromatic inner line, Van Eps's outer-voice-anchor principle in operation (Sustained note describes the scale). The signature left-hand demand of the chapter is the single-finger chromatic walk, where one finger must smoothly sound two or three successive half steps while the pedal stays held (Same finger across half steps), and the chapter notes that minor modes require their own treatment rather than transposition of major-key forms because the minor third interval refuses to parallel the major (Minor modes can't parallel major). Functional/contextual chord labeling is also established—the same voicing's symbol depends on its use in a progression (Chord symbol depends on use).

# Chapter 8 — Super and Sub Series: First-Inversion Triads (Major, Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch08.md`._

Chapter 8 introduces the 'super' and 'sub' vocabulary that powers the rest of Volume 1, defining them as diatonic-step displacements—a voice raised one scale step is in super position, lowered one step is in sub position (Super and sub defined)—then immediately reframing them as voice motion rather than static placement, so each super or sub event is read as a melodic occurrence in the chromatic inner line (Super/sub motion as voice motion). The load-bearing structural rule is the inversion-membership shift: every super or sub motion lands the triad on another named inversion in the family (first-inversion middle-voice super becomes second-inversion; first-inversion upper-voice sub becomes root, etc.), making the series mathematically self-contained (Inversion membership shift). To keep the system legible, the 'original inversion is the boss' doctrine requires the student to hold the named source inversion in mind at all times because identical notations can carry different functional meaning depending on which inversion caused them—a 'distinction without a difference' analogous to enharmonic spelling (Original inversion is the boss; Distinction without a difference). The generative axiom that 'all chords are centers' frames every voicing as both a destination and a launchpad for new centers (All chords are centers). Practice discipline is enforced by the ear-memory argument that wrong notes plant subconscious habits, mandating slow, correct first readings (Wrong notes plant bad habits) and bidirectional reading of one-direction notation (Repetition in fingering necessary); the chapter's set-jumping notation is justified as hand-shift-accuracy training (Alternating string sets purpose), and three-finger triads are preferred over barres in contexts where upcoming voice motion would be locked by a barre (Three-finger triads rationale).

# Chapter 9 — Super and Sub Series: Second-Inversion Triads (Major, Harmonic Minor, Melodic Minor)

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch09.md`._

Chapter 9 extends the super/sub series to second-inversion triads while introducing two structural shortcuts and a precise articulation mechanic. The duplication principle—first-inversion triads with upper voice sub produce the exact same notation as root triads with upper voice super, only landing the tonic on a different scale step—is what permits Van Eps to later prune redundant root-position upper-voice-super studies (Sub duplicates root super), and a closely related observation notes the audible by-product that upper-voice-sub motion produces root triads on the strong beats of each measure (Sub upper voice produces root). The technical heart of the chapter is the four-string articulated triad: the single melodic step in the moving upper line is distributed across two strings instead of one, yielding a four-string overall spread (Four-string spread mechanics), with the listener-side correctness test that only three tones should sound at any instant—never four—unless a hold-over is intended (Articulation timing precision). Execution is specified at joint level: the first joint of the second finger plays the double stop flattened, then arches into hammer position at the precise moment while the middle note on the D string is held full value (Second-finger hammer mechanic), and the coordinated raising of that flattened joint with the lowering of the first finger must behave as if 'mechanically linked' so the fingers cannot both be up or down simultaneously (Mechanical linkage thought line). Real-world flexibility is sanctioned by authorizing mixed four-string and three-string fingerings within a single passage for smoothness (Mixing fingerings for smoothness), and the chapter's articulation ethos is distilled into the rule that articulating fingers must work as a precision team rather than as independent units (Articulating fingers as team), with bidirectional practice of every articulated triad explicitly mandated (Practice articulation reversed).

# Chapter 10 — Super and Sub Series: Root-Position Triads and Open-Voicing Alternatives

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch10.md`._

Chapter 10 is the long capstone on root-position triads and open-voicing alternatives within the super/sub series, and it opens with two decoding rules the reader must internalize to access the compressed notation: voice changes are written every four keys of the cycle of fifths rather than all twelve, leaving the remaining fingerings to be reconstructed by the student (Four-key voice change shorthand), and a range-compensation rule that adds a step at the high end whenever one is lost at the low end (and vice versa) when the fingerboard runs out (Step loss compensated at range edges). The chapter restates and applies the cause/effect labeling rule from chapter 8—second-inversion lower-voice super shares notation with root-triad lower-voice sub but must be re-labeled depending on which inversion is the conceptual origin (Notation identity across inversions)—and defends including impractical-reach root voicings on conceptual-symmetry grounds, since 'what is done to one must be done to the others' even when reaches become extended (Voice motion mandates inclusion). A practical audible test distinguishes original notations from compromise fingerings: the difference is heard when tones are sustained, but vanishes when the moving voice is detached (Compromise vs original notation). Practice is governed by cycle-of-fifths transposition order to preserve continuity (Transpose by cycle of fifths), station-to-station drilling across all scale forms in both directions (Station-to-station scale practice), and octave-displacement work targeted at the 'golden asset' of picking up a phrase an octave higher or lower on demand (Octave transposition as mental drill). For the open-voicing material, individual-finger fingerings are preferred over barres and small double stops precisely because ongoing voice motion would be locked by a barre (Open voicing fingering practice), and the variation exercises are flagged as independent-finger-control drills rather than mere voicing catalogs (Independent finger control variation).

# Chapter 11 — Super and Sub Series: Open Voicings, Multi-Voice Motion and Harmonic Variation

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch11.md`._

Chapter 11 trains the Super and Sub Series of open voicings as a multi-voice motion discipline: while two voices move, a third must be physically sustained full value, with release of the held voice permitted only as a last resort (Sustaining voices through long reaches). Van Eps's lap-piano texture is enforced through specific 7-string left-hand mechanics—most notably the arched-2nd-finger sustain that holds the middle voice through open-voicing motion (Arched-finger sustain technique)—and through the cross (+) notational convention marking the deleted middle voice of the first-inversion triad that begins each station (Finger-team articulation marking). The chapter frames these drills not as isolated chord-shape practice but as the analytical foundation for improvisation, insisting that hearing and analyzing the voice motion is co-equal with executing it cleanly (Hearing voice motion beats physical execution; Voice motion mastery as improvisation prerequisite). Load-bearing standing instructions recur: every closed-to-open voicing study must be transposed across major and both minor forms and run in both directions (Transpose to all keys, both minors); single steps should be looped as finger-control drills (Repeating steps for finger control); finger-team articulation must transfer from harmonic minor to melodic minor, mixed minor, and major (Apply team principles to all scales); and the student must remain alert that identical fingerings carry different harmonic and notational meaning across the 7-string string-sets (Subtle differences across sets).

# Chapter 12 — Visualizing Harmonic Situations / The Magic of the Chromatic Scale, Contrary Motion Chromatic Triads, 5th Finger Principle

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch12.md`._

Chapter 12 lays down Van Eps's visualization and notation infrastructure and introduces his signature one-finger-two-notes left-hand technique. The chapter opens with the method's core left-hand philosophy—long reaches come from relaxed power and control, not anatomy (Long reaches require relaxed power)—then defines fingerboard cognition as geographic shape-recognition with multiple harmonic modes stacked in layers on the same physical space (Visualizing harmonic situations; Layered harmonic modes). The chromatic scale is named as the universal source containing every scale, arpeggio, and chord in all twelve keys, justifying why chromatic drills dominate the volume (Chromatic scale as universal source). Critical reading conventions are codified: per-bar/per-register accidentals are used instead of key signatures because the studies belong to no key (Accidentals vs key signatures), and the 7-string-specific string-set syntax is defined in full, including the broken (1/B3, B1/3) and divided (D1/4, 1/D3) set vocabulary required to parse every subsequent voicing (String set symbol system; Broken and divided sets defined). Practice doctrine is explicit—hard shots first so easy shots take care of themselves (Practice hard shots, easy shots follow), intentionally awkward long shifts as duplicate-location pedagogy (Intentional long shifts), and cross-exercise generalization of every principle (Principles transfer across exercises)—culminating in the chapter's signature contribution, the Fifth Finger Principle: angling and arching the first finger so its tip and the upper inside of its third joint sound two notes on non-adjacent frets simultaneously, unlocking the open-voicing arsenal (Fifth Finger Principle definition).

# Chapter 13 — Chromatics / Triads / Arpeggios — Major and Minor Series, Circle of Fifths, Contrary Motion

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch13.md`._

Chapter 13 drills chromatic triad/bass studies in Major and Minor Series cycled through the circle of fifths and contrary motion, generated by what Van Eps calls mathematical mechanical motion without regard for the harmonic result (Mechanical motion regardless of harmonic result). The pedagogical mandate is to find and practice nontraditional configurations rather than the well-known ones (Nontraditional configurations must be practiced) and to refuse to flinch from the inevitable clashes—dissonance is a feature of chromatic contrary motion and pedal-bass writing, not a defect to correct (Don't shy from dissonance; Dissonance is natural in contrary motion). The load-bearing analytical premise is that bass and triad are independent voices that incidentally coalesce into four-part chords: the bass operates as an independent fourth voice with its own accidentals, often in a different key from the triad, producing a mixed-accidental enharmonic texture the student must read carefully (Bass as independent fourth voice; Triad and bass as separate identities). Van Eps's preferred generator is the descending circle-of-fifths bass because of its modal neutrality (Cycle of fifths bass is neutral), exploited via the major-makes-minor / minor-makes-major identity that lets a 7th chord be heard as a triad of the opposite modality over a bass (Major makes minor, minor makes major), with inversion arpeggios used to surface which familiar four-note chord is actually present (Inversion arpeggios reveal hidden chords) and the 'chameleon' multi-naming doctrine emphasizing that any voicing has many callings under varied conditions (Chameleon chords). The chapter introduces the bass-suffix chord-symbol notation (e.g., 3B for third-in-bass) without which the Major/Minor Series labels are unreadable (Chord symbol bass-note suffix) and enforces the two-stage discipline rule: strict adherence to the format across all twelve keys first, deviation only after the principle is internalized (Discipline of strict format).

# Chapter 14 — Chromatics — Triads — Blockouts: Explanations, Charts and Blockout Patterns

_Distillation; backed by `chapters/ch14.md`._

Chapter 14 introduces and exhaustively charts the 'blockout,' Van Eps's term for a graphic two-line study in which a series of major and minor triads in root, first, and second inversions runs on the upper line against a completely independent bass on the lower line (Blockout definition; Bass and triad as independent voices). The chapter's central premise is that the recurring chord/bass misalignment is the curriculum: the resulting harmonic clashes are intentional and useful, and the student must not 'fix' them during the learning stage (Dissonance is intentional in blockouts; Strict adherence in learning stage). Two organizing taxonomies are defined—'repeat order,' in which the three inversions hold fixed positions in a per-measure pattern as keys change (Repeat order vs mixed order), and 'mixed order,' a scrambled inversion sequence whose cycles can run more than a hundred steps before repeating (Mixed-order patterns)—serving the chapter's analytical mission of systematically enumerating progression possibilities under controlled triad/bass independence (Purpose of blockouts). An explicit construction recipe is provided (three keys a major third apart, repeat-order inversions, chromatic bass from any starting step) so the student can build blockouts of their own (How to block out three keys). Reading conventions are flagged as load-bearing: descending chromatic bass lines are spelled with flats while triads carry their own key accidentals, producing enharmonic spellings that may make a third look like a second but keep the two lines visually isolated (Enharmonic spelling isolates lines). Fingerings and string-set markings are deliberately omitted so the student must finger every blockout in as many positions as possible (Why fingerings are omitted), and once the strict mathematical form is mastered, the chapter authorizes specific deviation techniques—repeat the chord while the bass continues, repeat the bass while the chords continue, skip steps in either cycle, splice the more pleasing sections of different cycles, or move one line by a whole tone or sustain it across a beat to tame an unwanted clash (When to break the strict form; Avoiding unwanted dissonance).

      "id": "van-eps:harmonic-mechanisms-vol-1:four-part-chord-construction",
      "name": "Four-Part Chord Construction (Triads to Extended Chords)",
      "summary": "Van Eps builds the harmonic vocabulary sequentially: triads first, then tetrads (seventh chords), then extended chords (9ths, 11ths, 13ths), grouped by chord-quality category (major, minor, dominant, diminished, augmented, half-diminished). Every triad is presented in all inversions on every string set, then 'completed' by adding a fourth voice to convert it to a tetrad. The system treats extended chords as substitution-expansions of their parent tetrads: a Cmaj9 is taught as Cmaj7 with the 9th replacing or doubling an inner voice.",
      "members": [
        {
          "id": "triad-inversions",

          "id": "extended-chords",

        }
